intro:
Reuben (Hebrew Re'uven, understood as "behold, a son") was the firstborn son of Jacob by Leah, and the ancestor of the tribe that bore his name. His story is one of squandered privilege: born the heir to the birthright and the natural leader among his brothers, he forfeited that preeminence through a grave sin, so that his life became a sober lesson in how the firstborn's blessing can be lost.

Leah named him at his birth in gratitude and hope: "Because the LORD has looked upon my affliction; for now my husband will love me" (Genesis 29:32). As the eldest of the twelve, Reuben shows flashes of conscience and tenderness. When the brothers plotted to kill Joseph, it was Reuben who intervened to save his life, persuading them to cast him into a pit rather than shed his blood, secretly intending to rescue him and restore him to their father (Genesis 37:21–22); his anguish when he found the pit empty is vividly recorded (Genesis 37:29–30). Later, in Egypt, he offered his own two sons as surety for Benjamin's safe return (Genesis 42:37).

Yet Reuben's defining act was his sin: he "went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine, and Israel heard of it" (Genesis 35:22). This violation of his father's bed — an assault on the family's honor and possibly a bid for dominance — cost him the birthright. On his deathbed Jacob acknowledged him as "my firstborn, my might, and the firstfruits of my strength," yet pronounced over him the solemn sentence, "Unstable as water, you shall not have preeminence, because you went up to your father's bed" (Genesis 49:3–4). The Chronicler later explains that because Reuben defiled his father's couch, "his birthright was given to the sons of Joseph," while the leadership passed to Judah (1 Chronicles 5:1).

The tribe of Reuben bore out the prophecy. Along with Gad and half of Manasseh, the Reubenites — being rich in cattle — asked for and received their inheritance east of the Jordan, on the high plateau of the Transjordan, pledging first to help the other tribes conquer Canaan (Numbers 32:1–5). Living apart across the river, Reuben never rose to prominence; the Song of Deborah rebukes the tribe for hesitating, "there were great searchings of heart," when Israel was called to battle (Judges 5:15–16). Exposed on the frontier, Reuben was among the first tribes carried into exile by Assyria. Yet the tribe is not forgotten by God: Reuben is named first among the sealed tribes of Israel in Revelation 7:5, a token that even forfeited privilege is gathered up in the mercy of God.
hitchcock_meaning: who sees the son; the vision of the son
